name: Pine Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Pine. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Pine surface cleaning without charring

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength for optimal Pine wood absorption

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise Pine surface treatment

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient Pine surface cleaning

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal from Pine

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform Pine cleaning

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete Pine surface treatment

overlapRatio.description: Beam overlap percentage for uniform Pine surface coverage

dwellTime.description: Optimal dwell time per spot for Pine cleaning efficiency

power.description: Typical range for industrial cleaning of softwoods like pine to remove contaminants without charring; starting low prevents thermal damage. Max based on avoiding substrate pyrolysis. Source: Studies on laser cleaning of wood in Journal of Cultural Heritage (e.g., 2015 paper on 1064 nm Nd:YAG for heritage wood, adapted for industrial use); manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics for similar applications.

scan_speed.description: Slower speeds for heavy contamination on pine (e.g., resins or dirt), faster for light; optimal balances efficiency and heat dissipation given pine's low thermal conductivity (~0.12 W/mK). Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Association guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., CleanLaser systems, recommending 500-2000 mm/s for organics).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred over ps/fs for cost-effective cleaning of pine; shorter pulses minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ) to <50 μm, avoiding charring. Ps/fs for ultra-precision but overkill for industrial. Source: Laser physics research in Optics & Lasers in Engineering (e.g., 2018 review on pulsed laser ablation of wood at 1064 nm).

repetition_rate.description: Mid-range for effective contaminant vaporization on pine; higher rates increase speed but risk heat accumulation due to pine's poor conductivity, leading to discoloration. Trade-off favors moderate for safety. Source: Academic literature in Applied Surface Science (e.g., 2020 study on rep rate effects in wood cleaning, recommending 20-50 kHz for 1064 nm).

energy_density.description: Fluence below pine's ablation threshold (~8-12 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe removal of surface contaminants; higher risks carbonization. Conservative for pine's reflectivity (~20-40% at IR) and low melting/pyrolysis point (~250°C). Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (e.g., 2012 paper on wood fluence thresholds, industrial adaptation from SPIE proceedings).

spot_size.description: Smaller for fine surface preparation on pine, larger for broad industrial areas; 200 μm optimal for uniform energy distribution without pinpoint damage. Source: Equipment specifications from manufacturers like Coherent and Trumpf lasers for cleaning heads (typical 150-300 μm for organic substrates).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy (e.g., paint or rust on pine); 2 passes recommended to achieve complete removal while monitoring for thermal effects. Source: Industry practice in case studies from Laser Focus World (e.g., 2019 industrial wood prep examples) and operator manuals.

overlap_ratio.description: Higher overlap ensures uniform coverage on irregular pine surfaces, preventing streaks; 70% balances efficiency and overlap-induced heating. Source: Process optimization studies in Optics Express (e.g., 2017 paper on scan strategies for laser cleaning, recommending 60-80% for woods).
